title: JavaScript var, let & const — Mastery Quiz
description: A comprehensive quiz covering scope, hoisting, the temporal dead zone, reassignment, mutation, and best practices for JavaScript variable declarations.

questions.question: What type of scope does `var` use?
questions.explanation: `var` is function-scoped, meaning it is accessible anywhere within the function it is declared in — including inside nested if blocks, for loops, or any other block within that function.

questions.question: What type of scope do `let` and `const` use?
questions.explanation: `let` and `const` are block-scoped. A block is any pair of curly braces `{}`, including if statements, for loops, while loops, and bare blocks.

questions.question: What does this code log to the console?
questions.explanation: `var` is function-scoped, so `x` is accessible throughout the entire `test` function, even outside the `if` block where it was declared. It logs `10`.

questions.question: What does this code log to the console?
questions.explanation: `let` is block-scoped. The variable `x` is confined to the `if` block's `{}` and is not accessible outside it. Attempting to access it throws a ReferenceError.

questions.question: What does this code log?
questions.explanation: Because `var` is function-scoped, all three setTimeout callbacks share the same `i` variable. By the time they execute, the loop has finished and `i` is `3`. All three log `3`.

questions.question: What does this code log?
questions.explanation: `let` creates a new binding of `i` for each loop iteration. Each setTimeout callback captures its own independent copy of `i`, so they correctly log `0`, `1`, and `2`.

questions.question: When a `var` declaration is hoisted, what value does the variable hold before its assignment line is reached?
questions.explanation: `var` declarations are hoisted to the top of their function and automatically initialized to `undefined`. The assignment stays in place, so accessing a `var` before its assignment line gives `undefined`, not an error.

questions.question: What does this code log?
questions.explanation: The `var x` declaration is hoisted to the top and initialized as `undefined`. The first `console.log` sees `undefined` because the assignment hasn't run yet. After `x = 5`, the second log shows `5`.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes how `let` is hoisted?
options: It is not hoisted at all
It is hoisted and initialized to undefined
It is hoisted but not initialized — placed in the temporal dead zone
It is hoisted and initialized to null
questions.explanation: `let` IS hoisted to the top of its block, but unlike `var`, it is NOT initialized. It sits in the temporal dead zone (TDZ) until the declaration line is reached. Accessing it before that line throws a ReferenceError.

questions.question: What happens when this code runs?
options: Works fine — class declarations are fully hoisted like function declarations
TypeError: MyClass is not a constructor
questions.explanation: Class declarations are subject to the temporal dead zone, just like `let` and `const`. Even though the class is hoisted, it cannot be accessed before its declaration line. This throws a ReferenceError.

questions.question: What is the temporal dead zone (TDZ)?
options: The time between two setTimeout calls
The period between when a let/const variable is hoisted and when its declaration line executes
A special scope created inside async functions
The gap between when a module loads and when it executes
questions.explanation: The TDZ is the gap between the start of a block (where `let`/`const` is hoisted) and the line where it is declared. During this window, any access to the variable throws a ReferenceError.

questions.question: What does this code produce?
questions.explanation: Inside the block, `a` is hoisted but enters the TDZ immediately. The first `console.log(a)` executes before the `let a = 42` line, so `a` is in the TDZ and a ReferenceError is thrown.

questions.question: Which keyword(s) allow you to reassign a variable to a new value?
questions.explanation: Both `var` and `let` allow reassignment. `const` does not — attempting to reassign a `const` variable throws a TypeError.

questions.question: What does this code log?
questions.explanation: `const` prevents reassignment of the binding, not mutation of the value. `arr` still points to the same array object — you just can't point `arr` at a different array. Mutating the array's contents with `.push()` works fine.

questions.question: What happens here?
questions.explanation: This is reassignment — pointing `arr` at a completely new array. `const` prohibits this. The original mutation example (`.push()`) works because the binding itself doesn't change. Here, we try to change the binding, which throws a TypeError.

questions.question: You want an object that is truly immutable — its properties cannot be changed. What is the correct approach?
options: Use const — it makes the object immutable
Use let with a type annotation
Use Object.freeze() in addition to const
There is no way to make a JavaScript object immutable
questions.explanation: `const` only prevents reassigning the variable. To prevent mutation of the object's properties, you need `Object.freeze()`. Note: `freeze()` is shallow — nested objects inside a frozen object are still mutable.

questions.question: Which keyword attaches to the global `window` object when declared at the top level in a browser?
questions.explanation: A `var` declared at the top level (outside any function) becomes a property of the global `window` object in browsers. `let` and `const` declared at the top level do NOT attach to `window` — they live in the script's own scope.

questions.question: What does this code do?
options: SyntaxError: Identifier 'x' has already been declared
questions.explanation: `let` does not allow re-declaration within the same scope. This is a SyntaxError that is caught before the code even runs. `var`, by contrast, would allow this silently.

questions.question: What is the recommended default keyword to use when declaring variables in modern JavaScript?
options: const — use unless you know you need to reassign
It doesn't matter; they all behave the same
questions.explanation: The modern best practice is to default to `const`. It signals that the binding won't change, helps the JS engine optimize, and makes your intent clear. Switch to `let` only when you need to reassign. Avoid `var` in new code.

questions.question: Why is the TDZ considered a feature rather than a bug?
options: It makes code run faster by skipping initialization
It throws a clear error when you access a variable before declaration, instead of silently returning undefined like var does
It allows variables to be shared between modules
It prevents variables from being garbage collected too early
questions.explanation: With `var`, accessing a variable before its declaration silently returns `undefined`, hiding bugs. The TDZ makes `let`/`const` throw a ReferenceError immediately, giving you a clear, actionable error that points directly to the problem.
